_meta.description: L3 mastery gate eval set. 50 questions over latest available 10-K filings for S&P 100 issuers.
_meta.authoring_principle: Questions written from general 10-K domain knowledge BEFORE running the pipeline. NOT shaped by what the retriever happens to fetch well. Some questions may be unanswerable from a given filing; the system MUST refuse rather than confabulate (context_recall is what catches this).
_meta.ground_truth_provenance: Written from public knowledge as of model cutoff (2026-01). Specifics may diverge from any individual filing — that divergence shows up as context_recall variance, not silent miscalibration.

questions.question: What was Apple's total net sales in its most recent fiscal year, and which product category contributed the largest share?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Apple reported total net sales of approximately $383B in FY2023, with iPhone the largest segment at roughly $200B (about 52% of revenue). Services was second at ~$85B.

questions.question: Who is Microsoft's independent registered public accounting firm and how long has the relationship been disclosed?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Deloitte & Touche LLP serves as Microsoft's independent registered public accounting firm. The firm has audited Microsoft's consolidated financial statements for many years; the 10-K discloses the auditor's PCAOB ID and the year the engagement began.

questions.question: How is Alphabet's voting structure organized between Class A, Class B, and Class C shares?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Class A common stock has one vote per share, Class B has ten votes per share (held by founders and insiders), and Class C has zero votes per share. The dual-class structure concentrates voting power with the founders despite their economic minority.

questions.question: What does Meta disclose as its primary source of revenue?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Meta's revenue is overwhelmingly from advertising on Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and third-party affiliated websites and applications. Family of Apps advertising represents the majority of total revenue; Reality Labs is a small and loss-making segment.

questions.question: What end-market segments does NVIDIA report, and which one drove the largest year-over-year revenue growth in the most recent filing?
questions.ground_truth_answer: NVIDIA reports Data Center, Gaming, Professional Visualization, Automotive, and OEM/Other segments. Data Center, driven by demand for AI training and inference accelerators (Hopper architecture), drove the largest YoY growth in the most recent 10-K.

questions.question: How much capex did Amazon report for property and equipment in the most recent fiscal year?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Amazon reported tens of billions in property and equipment additions, primarily for fulfillment, AWS infrastructure, and transportation network. The most recent 10-K disclosed approximately $50-55B in cash outflows for purchases of property and equipment.

questions.question: What is JPMorgan Chase's CET1 capital ratio at year-end and what is the regulatory minimum required?
questions.ground_truth_answer: JPMorgan reported CET1 capital ratio of approximately 15% at year-end, well above the regulatory minimum (Standardized approach minimum including buffers is around 11.5-12.5% for JPM as a G-SIB). The exact figures are disclosed in the regulatory capital section of the 10-K.

questions.question: What was the financial impact of the Kenvue separation on Johnson & Johnson's continuing operations?
questions.ground_truth_answer: J&J completed the separation of its Consumer Health business as Kenvue in 2023. The 10-K reports Kenvue results as discontinued operations through the separation date and discloses the gain on separation, the impact on shareholders' equity from the share exchange, and the resulting reduction in revenue base going forward.

questions.question: What does Pfizer disclose about expected revenue decline from COVID-19 products?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Pfizer's 10-K discusses the wind-down of COVID-19 vaccine (Comirnaty) and oral antiviral (Paxlovid) revenues from peak pandemic levels, with substantial year-over-year declines and a transition to a commercial/private market model in the U.S. Forward guidance reflects this normalization.

questions.question: What is Berkshire Hathaway's largest equity holding by fair value at the latest balance sheet date?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Apple is Berkshire's largest single equity investment by fair value, comprising a substantial fraction of the equity portfolio. The 10-K's investments footnote discloses the cost and fair value of the top equity positions.

questions.question: What does Tesla disclose about deliveries by region in the most recent 10-K?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Tesla discloses unit deliveries split between Model 3/Y and Model S/X, with revenue geographic split among United States, China, and Other International. China is one of the largest international markets; the U.S. remains the single largest geographic source of automotive revenue.

questions.question: What are ExxonMobil's reported proved reserves at year-end?
questions.ground_truth_answer: ExxonMobil's 10-K reports total proved reserves on an oil-equivalent basis (typically in the range of 16-17 billion BOE), with breakdown across crude oil, natural gas liquids, and natural gas. The reserve report is in the Supplemental Information on Oil and Gas Activities.

questions.question: How many Walmart stores and clubs operate worldwide and how is the store count split between U.S. and international?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Walmart operates approximately 10,500 stores worldwide; roughly 4,600 are U.S. Walmart stores, around 600 are Sam's Club U.S. locations, and the remainder (over 5,000) are international units across Mexico, Central America, Canada, Africa, India and other markets.

questions.question: What is Visa's net revenue for the most recent fiscal year and what proportion comes from international transaction revenue?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Visa reported net revenue around $33B in FY2023. International transaction revenue (cross-border, FX) is roughly a third of net revenue and is the highest-margin component because of FX spread economics.

questions.question: What are UnitedHealth's two reportable segments and their relative size?
questions.ground_truth_answer: UnitedHealth reports UnitedHealthcare (insurance) and Optum (health services). UnitedHealthcare contributes the majority of consolidated revenue; Optum has been growing faster and now contributes over a third of revenue (with Optum Rx, Optum Health, and Optum Insight as sub-segments).

questions.question: What is Home Depot's stated capital allocation priority in the most recent 10-K?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Home Depot's stated capital allocation priorities are: (1) reinvest in the business, (2) pay a growing dividend, and (3) return excess cash to shareholders via share repurchases. The 10-K describes the capital framework in MD&A and executive overview sections.

questions.question: What is Mastercard's reported gross dollar volume (GDV) and how does that relate to its net revenue?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Mastercard reports gross dollar volume in the multi-trillion dollar range; net revenue is derived from a small effective yield on GDV through domestic assessment fees, cross-border fees, and transaction processing fees, plus value-added services. The take rate is well below 100bps of GDV.

questions.question: What does Bank of America disclose as its total deposits at year-end?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Bank of America discloses total deposits in the consolidated balance sheet (typically $1.9 trillion range), with breakdown between non-interest-bearing and interest-bearing, and across consumer, wealth, and global banking segments.

questions.question: What dividend per share did Chevron pay in the most recent fiscal year?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Chevron paid an aggregate dividend per share of approximately $6 in FY2023 (quarterly $1.51 by year-end). The 10-K's selected financial data and dividend policy sections disclose the per-share amounts and total cash returned.

questions.question: What does Coca-Cola report as the largest geographic operating segment by revenue?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Coca-Cola's largest geographic operating segment is North America, followed by EMEA (Europe, Middle East and Africa) and Latin America. The Bottling Investments and Global Ventures segments are smaller and reported separately.

questions.question: Across Apple's risk factors and MD&A, what does the company identify as its single largest geographic concentration risk and how does management say it is mitigating that risk?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Apple identifies China (manufacturing concentration through Foxconn and other partners, plus a large end-market for product sales) as a major geographic concentration risk. Management discusses diversification of manufacturing into India and Vietnam, multi-sourcing of components, and ongoing geopolitical and regulatory monitoring. The risk and mitigation are split between Item 1A and Item 7.

questions.question: Combine Microsoft's segment results with its risk factors — which segment is most exposed to AI capex risk and what does management quantify about depreciation timing?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Microsoft's Intelligent Cloud segment (Azure) is most exposed to AI capex risk. Management discusses extending the useful lives of server and networking equipment from 4 to 6 years (an accounting estimate change), discloses the multi-billion-dollar reduction in depreciation expense from the change, and quantifies AI infrastructure capex commitments going forward.

questions.question: Reconcile NVIDIA's customer concentration disclosures with its supply chain disclosures — which named customer or region creates the most concentrated revenue exposure and where is that disclosed?
questions.ground_truth_answer: NVIDIA discloses that a small number of large hyperscaler customers account for a substantial share of Data Center revenue (typically with one or two customers each individually representing more than 10% of revenue, named in the customers footnote). Supply concentration is on TSMC for foundry capacity. Both customer and supplier concentrations are highlighted as material risk factors.

questions.question: What does Alphabet disclose about ongoing antitrust litigation in both the legal proceedings and risk factors sections, and is there a quantified financial exposure or just qualitative risk?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Alphabet discloses material antitrust litigation in the U.S. (DOJ Search and Ad Tech cases) and EU (Google Shopping, Android, AdSense). Risk factors describe potential remedies including structural changes; legal proceedings discuss the procedural posture. Most financial impact is qualitative; loss contingency reserves are disclosed where probable and estimable per ASC 450.

questions.question: What does Meta disclose about Reality Labs operating losses and how does management explain the persistence of those losses in the long-term capital allocation discussion?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Reality Labs has reported multi-year operating losses well in excess of $10B annually. Management frames this as a long-horizon investment in next-generation computing platforms (AR/VR/MR), and links it to research and product investments that are not expected to generate near-term operating income. The MD&A and CEO letter discuss the persistence of losses against an explicit multi-year investment thesis.

questions.question: Connect Amazon's segment operating income disclosures with its capital expenditure footnotes — which segment generates most of consolidated operating income and which segment absorbs most of the capex?
questions.ground_truth_answer: AWS generates the majority of consolidated operating income (often the entire operating income, with Retail being thin or modestly negative). AWS also absorbs the largest share of property and equipment additions due to data center buildout for AI workloads. The asymmetry — small revenue share, large profit and capex share — is a recurring theme in MD&A.

questions.question: Synthesize Tesla's gross margin trajectory disclosure with its production ramp risk factors — what does management identify as the primary cause of margin pressure and what cost actions are mentioned?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Tesla discusses pricing actions taken to stimulate demand, with associated gross margin compression in automotive, partly offset by manufacturing cost reductions on Model 3/Y and the ramp of newer products. Risk factors cite raw material costs (lithium, nickel) and production launch costs. Cost actions discussed include factory throughput improvements, vertical integration, and supplier renegotiation.

questions.question: What does JPMorgan disclose about its allowance for credit losses methodology and how is the allowance balance reconciled to the income statement provision in MD&A?
questions.ground_truth_answer: JPMorgan discloses CECL methodology — lifetime expected losses on amortized cost loans, with quantitative and qualitative components reflecting macro forecasts. The allowance roll-forward in the credit footnote ties to the provision for credit losses on the income statement, with separate disclosure of net charge-offs and reserve build/release. MD&A discusses macroeconomic assumptions driving the period's provision.

questions.question: Reconcile ExxonMobil's reserve replacement ratio, capex on upstream, and DD&A — does cash investment indicate growth, maintenance, or decline?
questions.ground_truth_answer: ExxonMobil's upstream capital and exploration expenditures funded both maintenance of existing production and growth projects (Permian, Guyana, LNG). The reserve replacement ratio over multi-year averages combined with reported DD&A and capex levels indicate a portfolio sustaining or modestly growing reserves and production. The supplemental oil and gas information discloses the underlying reserve adds, revisions, and production.

questions.question: What does UnitedHealth disclose in its risk factors and legal proceedings about cybersecurity incidents and how are the costs treated in the financial statements?
questions.ground_truth_answer: UnitedHealth disclosed the Change Healthcare cyberattack as a material event, with risk factors flagging cybersecurity exposure and legal proceedings discussing customer and regulatory actions. Costs (notification, remediation, litigation, advances to providers) are recognized as incurred; management quantifies the incremental costs in the Optum segment commentary and discusses insurance recoveries where applicable.

questions.question: Combine Johnson & Johnson's litigation contingency disclosures with its cash flow statement — what does the talc-related litigation accrual and payment activity look like over the year?
questions.ground_truth_answer: J&J discloses material talc-related litigation, with reserves reflected in current and long-term liabilities. Cash flow effects are visible in operating cash flow as legal settlements and accruals. The 10-K provides a detailed legal proceedings section discussing the bankruptcy-strategy attempts, MDLs, and ongoing trials, plus the accrual amounts where probable and estimable.

questions.question: Synthesize Pfizer's R&D spend, pipeline disclosures, and goodwill — does the recent Seagen acquisition show up clearly across all three?
questions.ground_truth_answer: The Seagen acquisition (closed late 2023) drove a material increase in goodwill and intangible assets, with deal financing visible in long-term debt issuance. R&D spend reflects integration of Seagen's pipeline (ADC platform). The pipeline section of the business overview and the M&A footnote quantify the deal terms and the post-close R&D and goodwill changes.

questions.question: Reconcile Berkshire Hathaway's investment gains/losses with its operating earnings discussion — why does management de-emphasize quarterly investment gains?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Berkshire reports investment and derivative gains/losses through the income statement under ASC 321, which forces fair value changes through earnings even on long-held positions. Management de-emphasizes these because they reflect mark-to-market on a multi-hundred-billion-dollar portfolio and are not indicative of operating performance. The chairman's letter and 10-K caution readers to focus on operating earnings of the underlying businesses.

questions.question: Connect Walmart's e-commerce growth disclosures with its segment operating margin — has digital growth helped or hurt segment profitability over the year?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Walmart's e-commerce continued double-digit growth led by U.S. and India (Flipkart). Segment operating margin shows the mix shift — e-commerce has been historically dilutive to margin but Walmart discloses progress on advertising (Walmart Connect), marketplace, and fulfillment efficiency that have improved e-commerce contribution margin and partially offset the dilution.

questions.question: Combine Visa's litigation footnote with its stockholders equity — what does the U.S. interchange MDL settlement provision history suggest about future contingent exposure?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Visa's 10-K discloses the U.S. interchange multidistrict litigation, with a multi-billion-dollar Class B retrospective responsibility split between Visa and Mastercard, and an escrow funded from Class B stock conversion. The litigation footnote and stockholders equity show the connection: future opt-out claims could increase the provision, which would affect Class B conversion ratios and reduce Visa's remaining exposure.

questions.question: Synthesize Bank of America's net interest income trajectory with its deposit beta disclosure — what does management say about the asset sensitivity of the balance sheet?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Bank of America discloses NII sensitivity to a parallel shift in rates and discusses deposit beta (the pace at which deposit rates reprice with policy rates). The MD&A quantifies asset sensitivity (positive or negative bps to NII for a 100bp parallel shift) and discusses how deposit mix shifts (non-interest-bearing to interest-bearing) affect realized NII relative to model sensitivity.

questions.question: Connect Home Depot's same-store sales disclosure with its inventory commentary — what does management identify as the primary cause of any same-store sales weakness in the most recent year?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Home Depot disclosed pressure on same-store sales (comp ticket and comp transactions) attributed to softer big-ticket discretionary categories and sensitivity to interest-rate-driven housing turnover. Inventory commentary addresses inventory levels, shrink, and category mix; management discusses returning inventory to pre-pandemic productivity ratios and lapping prior-year lumber price effects.

questions.question: Reconcile Mastercard's cross-border volume growth with its net revenue mix — what does cross-border tell us about international travel recovery and high-margin revenue?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Mastercard reports cross-border volume growth well in excess of domestic, reflecting continued travel recovery and online cross-border commerce. Cross-border yields are higher than domestic processing yields, so the volume mix is accretive to net revenue and operating margin. MD&A explicitly links cross-border to operating leverage and currency volatility hedging.

questions.question: Combine Chevron's free cash flow and shareholder return disclosures — has the company returned more to shareholders than it generated in free cash flow over the year, and how was that funded?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Chevron discloses operating cash flow, capex, and free cash flow; combined with dividends and share repurchases, the 10-K shows whether shareholder returns exceeded FCF in a given year. In years where they did, the gap was funded from balance sheet liquidity (cash and short-term investments) rather than incremental long-term debt. The capital allocation framework targets a leverage ratio range.

questions.question: Synthesize Coca-Cola's bottling investment disclosures with its operating segment results — how does the refranchising strategy affect reported revenue, margin, and capital intensity?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Coca-Cola's refranchising of company-owned bottlers reduces consolidated revenue (concentrate model has lower revenue but higher margin), increases operating margin, and reduces capital intensity. The Bottling Investments segment captures the residual company-owned operations. MD&A discusses the structural margin uplift and cash flow conversion improvement from the refranchising program.

questions.question: Compare how Apple and Microsoft each describe their AI strategy and expected capital intensity in their respective most recent 10-Ks.
questions.ground_truth_answer: Apple frames AI primarily as on-device intelligence (Apple Silicon, Neural Engine, privacy-preserving), with capex absorbed within ordinary product hardware investment. Microsoft frames AI as Azure-led infrastructure plus Copilot product surfaces, with explicit multi-billion-dollar AI infrastructure capex commitments and an extended useful-life accounting change. The capital intensity differential is stark — Microsoft is committing to data-center-scale capex; Apple is monetizing through device sales.

questions.question: Compare the regulatory and antitrust risk disclosures in Alphabet's 10-K versus Meta's 10-K. Where do their disclosed exposures overlap and where do they diverge?
questions.ground_truth_answer: Both Alphabet and Meta disclose material EU regulatory exposure (DMA, DSA) and U.S. FTC/DOJ activity. Alphabet emphasizes Search/Ad Tech antitrust cases with potential structural remedies; Meta emphasizes platform-integrity, child-safety, and competition issues including the Instagram acquisition retrospective challenge. They overlap on EU privacy/data-flow risk and diverge on the specific remedies sought.

questions.question: Compare NVIDIA's and AMD's customer concentration and supply chain disclosures in their respective 10-Ks.
questions.ground_truth_answer: Both disclose foundry concentration on TSMC. NVIDIA discloses meaningful concentration in a small set of hyperscaler customers in Data Center revenue (each potentially >10% individually). AMD discloses more diversified customer base across PC OEMs, server, and embedded, with named PC OEMs (Dell, HP, Lenovo) as 10%+ customers in some years. NVIDIA's exposure is more concentrated in AI hyperscalers; AMD's in PC OEMs.

questions.question: Compare Apple's gross margin in its most recent fiscal year to the prior fiscal year, and identify what management attributes the change to.
questions.ground_truth_answer: Apple's gross margin expanded year over year, with services gross margin running materially above products gross margin. Management attributes the consolidated improvement to favorable mix shift toward services, cost actions on hardware, and stable commodity input costs. The MD&A breaks out products vs services gross margin and explains the drivers per category.

questions.question: Compare JPMorgan Chase and Bank of America on their CET1 capital ratios and their disclosed capital return plans in the most recent 10-K.
questions.ground_truth_answer: JPMorgan reported CET1 around 15%; BAC reported CET1 around 11.8%, both above their respective regulatory minimums (G-SIB plus stress capital buffer). Both 10-Ks describe planned capital return through dividends and share repurchases subject to CCAR and SCB outcomes. JPM's higher CET1 reflects both higher RWA growth absorption capacity and a different SCB.

questions.question: Compare ExxonMobil's and Chevron's capital allocation framework — share repurchases, dividends, and reinvestment — as disclosed in the most recent 10-Ks.
questions.ground_truth_answer: Both Exxon and Chevron describe through-cycle capital allocation: prioritize maintenance and growth capex within stated leverage targets, then a competitive and growing dividend, then share repurchases. Exxon emphasizes integrated portfolio and Permian growth; Chevron emphasizes Permian, Gulf, and emerging Guyana exposure. Repurchase authorization sizes and pace differ but the framework is similar.

questions.question: Compare Walmart's and Costco's reported membership/loyalty revenue economics as disclosed in the most recent 10-Ks.
questions.ground_truth_answer: Costco reports membership fees as a separate revenue line, contributing several billion dollars annually at very high margins (essentially flowing to operating income). Walmart discloses Walmart+ subscription growth qualitatively but does not break out membership revenue at the same granularity, and combines membership economics with general retail revenue. Costco's membership model is structurally more transparent and a larger share of operating income.

questions.question: Compare J&J's and Pfizer's R&D-to-revenue ratios and pipeline disclosure approaches in the most recent 10-K.
questions.ground_truth_answer: J&J reports R&D as a percentage of sales in pharmaceutical and medtech operations (typically high single digits to low teens of revenue at consolidated level). Pfizer's R&D ratio runs higher when its top-line is normalized excluding COVID, and the absolute R&D dollars are similar to J&J's pharmaceutical-only R&D. Pipeline disclosure: J&J groups by therapeutic area with named late-stage molecules; Pfizer reports by therapeutic area with explicit Phase status and indicates expected approvals/launches.

questions.question: Compare Visa's and Mastercard's cross-border revenue mix and pricing disclosures.
questions.ground_truth_answer: Both Visa and Mastercard derive a disproportionate share of revenue from cross-border (international) transaction processing relative to domestic, due to higher take rates on FX and incentive structures. Visa generally has slightly more concentration in domestic U.S. processing while Mastercard has historically had a slightly higher international mix. Both disclose pricing actions only at a high level given competitive sensitivity, and both disclose the structural cross-border yield premium.

questions.question: Compare Apple's and Alphabet's share repurchase activity and remaining authorization in the most recent fiscal year.
questions.ground_truth_answer: Both companies returned tens of billions of dollars through buybacks. Apple historically has a larger absolute repurchase program (~$80B+ annual pace at times) with a stated goal of reaching net-cash-neutral over time; Alphabet runs a meaningfully smaller absolute program (typically $50-70B annually) but expanded in recent years. Both 10-Ks disclose the remaining authorization after year-end.
